name: Emain Macha
description: Emain Macha, also known as Navan Fort, is an ancient ceremonial site near Armagh in Northern Ireland. It holds immense significance in Irish mythology and history, believed to have been the royal capital of the Ulaid kingdom, ruled by the legendary King Conchobar mac Nessa. Emain Macha is deeply intertwined with the Ulster Cycle of myths, a collection of heroic tales featuring figures like Cú Chulainn. The site is associated with the goddess Macha, who, according to legend, cursed the warriors of Ulster to suffer labour pains during their greatest need, stemming from her forced race against the king's horses while heavily pregnant. Archaeological evidence suggests the site was a significant religious and political center, featuring a large circular enclosure and a massive Iron Age structure known as the 'Great Mound' or 'Cráebruad.' The tales of Emain Macha depict a vibrant court, filled with warriors, druids, and poets, a place of feasting, contests, and strategic planning. It embodies the power, heroism, and tragedy characteristic of early Irish culture, serving as a symbolic heartland for the Ulaid people and a lasting testament to the rich oral traditions of Celtic Ireland. Emain Macha represents a powerful connection to the past, a landscape imbued with myth and legend, where the veil between the mortal and supernatural realms felt thin.
subtitle: Royal Seat of the Ulaid Kingdom

keyMyths.title: The Curse of Macha
keyMyths.description: Macha, forced to race while pregnant, curses the Ulstermen to suffer labour pains in their hour of need. This curse weakens them during the Táin Bó Cúailnge.

keyMyths.title: Foundation of Emain Macha
keyMyths.description: Macha outlines the fort with her brooch, marking the boundaries and establishing it as a significant place. The name means 'Macha's twins'.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
